old_claim.note: Pottery provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Al-Kindi's water-bath apparatus places the cucurbit inside an earthenware or pottery pot, directly enabling controlled moist heating without making ceramic material universal to every alembic component.

source_shape.source_locator: p. 88 specifies an earthenware or pottery pot filled with water and sized to the cucurbit as the vessel for moist-heat distillation.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The documented ninth-century water-bath unit directly uses a fired-clay outer vessel.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Pottery is enabling rather than required because it provides the cited bath vessel, while the same apparatus also contains glass and wooden components.

invariant_preserved_or_changed: Preserved and locked by graph invariant 2026-07-16-medieval-alembic-distillation-scope: pottery remains an enabling apparatus material with a precise locator.
would_reject_if: The Arabic terms in the cited passage are retranslated as a non-ceramic vessel.
The node is narrowed to dry distillation with no pottery water-bath apparatus.
A dedicated ceramic_laboratory_vessels node is added and should replace broad pottery.
short_reason: Al-Kindi's moist-heating unit explicitly uses an earthenware or pottery bath pot.
